questions.question: What does `Partial<T>` do and when should you use it?

choices.b: It makes every property of T optional (`T[K] | undefined`)
choices.c: It removes all optional properties from T
choices.d: It makes T deeply optional recursively

questions.question: What does `Required<T>` do?

choices.a: It adds runtime validation to required fields
choices.b: It makes every property of T required, removing any `?` optionality
choices.d: It forces all properties to be non-null

questions.question: What does `Pick<T, K>` do vs `Omit<T, K>`?

choices.a: `Pick` keeps only the listed keys from T; `Omit` removes the listed keys from T
choices.b: `Pick` removes the listed keys; `Omit` keeps them
choices.c: They work only with string types
choices.d: Pick and Omit require K to extend `string`

questions.question: What does `Record<K, V>` do?

choices.a: It creates an array of key-value pairs
choices.b: It creates an object type with keys of type K and values of type V
choices.c: It is identical to `{ [key: string]: V }`
choices.d: Record only works with string literal unions as keys

questions.question: What does `NonNullable<T>` do?

choices.a: It throws at runtime if the value is null
choices.b: It removes `null` and `undefined` from the type T

questions.explanation:
`NonNullable<T>` excludes `null` and `undefined` from a type:

```typescript
type NonNullable<T> = T & {};//conceptually: T extends null|undefined ? never : T

type A = NonNullable<string | null | undefined>; // string
type B = NonNullable<number | null>;             // number
type C = NonNullable<null | undefined>;          // never
```

Use cases:
ŌĆó After a null check in a generic context where TypeScript can't narrow
ŌĆó Expressing that a value has been validated as non-null
ŌĆó In mapped types to filter out nullable properties

```typescript
type RequiredValues<T> = { [K in keyof T]: NonNullable<T[K]> };
```

questions.question: What does `Parameters<T>` return?

choices.a: The number of parameters a function expects
choices.b: A tuple type of the parameter types of function type T
choices.c: An array of parameter names as strings

questions.question: What is `ReadonlyArray<T>` (or `readonly T[]`) and why use it for function parameters?

choices.a: It prevents sorting the array at compile time only
choices.b: It creates an array type where mutating methods (push, pop, sort) are removed from the type, signaling the function won't mutate the array
choices.c: It is identical to a regular array type at runtime and compile time
choices.d: `ReadonlyArray` makes new copies on every access
